agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled relative to its lifecycle states (placed, invoiced, shipped).","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, progressing through lifecycle states from placement to shipment."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order, preventing fulfillment and (presumably) triggering any necessary financial reversal."},{"term":"Invoicing","definition":"The generation of a financial document binding the customer to payment. Described by Finance as irreversible."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer, representing a point-of-no-return for fulfillment."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The initial act of creating an order. Operations states this is the moment invoicing occurs."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy from Product defining the cancellation window. A VP could change this window (e.g., restrict it further). However, this rule directly contradicts BR-2 + BR-3.","testable_assertion":"Given an order in any pre-shipment state, cancellation should be permitted."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled. Invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance states invoicing is irreversible — this implies a hard financial/accounting constraint. Reversing an invoice has legal, tax, and audit implications. If violated, financial records become inconsistent.","testable_assertion":"Given an order that has been invoiced, any attempt to cancel MUST be rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy decision about when to invoice. A VP could decide to defer invoicing to a later stage (e.g., at pick/pack). However, as currently stated, it means every placed order is immediately invoiced.","testable_assertion":"Given a newly placed order, an invoice MUST be generated at the moment of placement."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Customer places an order and immediately requests cancellation","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — BR-1 says allow (not yet shipped), BR-2 + BR-3 say deny (already invoiced at placement). This is the core contradiction."},{"scenario":"Order placed but invoice generation fails or is delayed","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — If the invoice hasn't been created yet, BR-1 would allow cancellation, but BR-3 says invoicing happens at placement. Is there a window between placement and invoice commit?"},{"scenario":"Partial shipment scenario","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — If an order has multiple line items and only some have shipped, the cancellation window is ambiguous even under BR-1 alone."},{"scenario":"Credit note or refund after invoicing","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — Finance says invoicing is irreversible, but standard accounting practice allows credit notes to offset invoices. If credit notes are permitted, cancellation-with-credit-note may be a valid path that reconciles all three rules."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High — if we implement BR-1 and allow cancellations of invoiced orders, we create invoice-payment mismatches, tax reporting errors, and potential audit failures. If we implement BR-2 strictly, no order can ever be cancelled, which increases refund processing costs and damages customer trust.","operational":"High — the team cannot build this feature until the contradiction is resolved. Any implementation will violate at least one stakeholder's requirement, creating rework risk.","user":"High — customers expect to cancel orders before shipment. If cancellation is effectively impossible (BR-2 + BR-3), customer satisfaction and retention will suffer significantly.","risk":"Critical — shipping code that violates financial invariants (BR-2) risks accounting/compliance issues. Shipping code that ignores the PM requirement (BR-1) means the feature doesn't exist. Either path has serious consequences.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders if BR-2+BR-3 prevail, leading to frustration, chargebacks, and support burden."},{"group":"Finance / Accounting","impact":"If BR-1 prevails and invoiced orders are cancelled, invoices become orphaned — audit trail breaks, revenue recognition errors, potential regulatory exposure."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Feature cannot ship as specified. Requires re-negotiation of requirements."},{"group":"Operations","impact":"May need to change invoicing timing (defer invoicing past placement) if the business decides cancellation must be supported."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the brunt of complaints regardless of which rule wins, needs a clear policy to communicate."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct logical contradiction. BR-1 permits cancellation before shipment. BR-2 forbids cancellation after invoicing. BR-3 states invoicing happens at placement. Taken together: every order is invoiced at placement, invoiced orders cannot be cancelled, therefore no order can ever be cancelled — directly contradicting BR-1. Acceptance criteria cannot be defined until stakeholders resolve this conflict. Confidence is ≤50 due to the contradiction.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and not a matter of interpretation — the three rules are logically inconsistent.","Invoicing irreversibility is likely a true financial invariant (accounting/tax/audit implications).","The resolution likely involves either deferring invoicing or introducing credit notes as a cancellation mechanism."],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Finance would accept credit notes as a workaround (cancel order + issue credit note against invoice).","Whether Operations can defer invoicing to a later lifecycle stage (e.g., at shipment).","Whether there is a transactional gap between placement and invoice commit that could serve as a cancellation window."],"assumptions":["All three stakeholder statements are current and authoritative.","Invoicing here means a legally binding financial document, not a pro-forma or draft.","There is no existing credit note or invoice reversal mechanism that stakeholders forgot to mention."]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements are mutually exclusive. If orders are invoiced at placement (Operations) and invoiced orders cannot be cancelled (Finance), then orders can never be cancelled after placement — directly contradicting the PM requirement that orders can be cancelled any time before shipment. The logical conjunction of all three rules yields: cancellation is both permitted (BR-1) and forbidden (BR-2+BR-3) for every order between placement and shipment.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance","Operations","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["(A) Defer invoicing — Operations changes policy to invoice at shipment or pick/pack, opening a cancellation window between placement and invoicing. BR-1 and BR-2 both hold; BR-3 is relaxed.","(B) Allow credit notes — Finance permits issuing a credit note against the invoice upon cancellation. The invoice is not reversed (BR-2 holds), but its financial effect is neutralized. BR-1 and BR-3 both hold; BR-2 is preserved in letter.","(C) Restrict cancellation window — PM accepts that cancellation is only possible before invoicing. If invoicing is at placement, this effectively means no cancellation. BR-2 and BR-3 hold; BR-1 is abandoned.","(D) Introduce invoice states — Finance distinguishes between 'provisional invoice' (reversible, at placement) and 'final invoice' (irreversible, at shipment). Cancellation allowed while invoice is provisional. All three rules hold in spirit."],"recommendation":"Option B (credit notes) or Option D (invoice states) are most likely to satisfy all stakeholders. Option B is standard accounting practice and preserves the irreversibility of invoices while enabling effective cancellation. Recommend a cross-functional meeting with Product, Finance, and Operations to align before any implementation begins."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot define acceptance criteria or implement this feature as specified.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 contradicts the combined effect of BR-2 and BR-3. Any implementation will violate at least one stated requirement."}]}
